text: The surgeon uses the Intuitive XI robot to perform a robotic right inguinal hernia repair. The camera port is placed at the umbilicus, and robotic trocars are inserted laterally on both sides of the abdomen. The ports are positioned close together, with a preference for lateral placement to minimize visible scarring.

The surgeon performs a robotic right inguinal hernia repair using the Intuitive XI robot. The camera port is placed at the umbilicus, and robotic ports are inserted laterally on both sides of the abdomen. The surgeon places the robotic trocar on one side, while the assistant places the contralateral trocar. The ports are positioned laterally for cosmetic reasons, minimizing visible scars from the frontal view.

The robotic system is moved into position, with the scrub tech adjusting the height to avoid collisions with the patient and equipment. The circulator aligns the robotic boom using laser crosshairs over the camera port at the umbilicus, assisted by the surgeon. The final port positioning is visible.

The robotic system is positioned over the camera port at the umbilicus using laser crosshairs for precise alignment. The scrub tech adjusts the height to prevent collisions, while the circulator moves the robotic boom into place. The fourth arm of the XI robot is moved aside, and the camera arm is attached, preparing for endoscope installation and targeting.

The fourth arm of the XI robot is repositioned away from the surgical field, while the camera arm is installed. The endoscope is inserted, and the robotic system guides the targeting process, directing the scope towards the dissection site. The robot adjusts its arms to optimize positioning for the procedure.

The robotic arms are positioned internally to target the site of dissection, ensuring they avoid collisions and reach the target anatomy. Under direct visualization, the robotic scissor is carefully inserted into the abdomen, with attention to the sharp tips to prevent injury. Once the scissor is placed, the docking process is completed.

The robotic arms are positioned to avoid collisions and reach the target anatomy. Under direct visualization, the robotic scissor is inserted into the abdomen, ensuring the sharp tips do not cause injury. The docking is completed, and the surgeon begins the operation by making a curvilinear incision anterior to the hernia defect. The incision extends medially to the median umbilical ligament, using sharp scissors, cautery scissors, or hook cautery.

The surgeon begins the operation at the console by making a curvilinear incision anterior to the defect using sharp scissors, cautery scissors, or hook cautery. The incision is extended medially to the median umbilical ligament, marking the medial border of the dissection. The dissection proceeds straight down the median umbilical ligament to identify the pubic tubercle, using primarily blunt dissection to avoid injury to surrounding structures.

The surgeon begins dissection in the preperitoneal space by working straight down the median umbilical ligament, using blunt dissection to identify the pubic tubercle. The pubic tubercle is a firm structure, with vascular structures immediately lateral to it, the spermatic cord anterior and lateral to those, and the indirect defect lateral to the cord structures. The transabdominal approach allows visualization of the cord structures by lifting the peritoneum.

The surgeon uses a transabdominal approach to visualize the spermatic cord and surrounding vascular structures. The peritoneum is lifted to expose these structures, and the peritoneal hernia sac is dissected free from the cord using primarily blunt dissection, with occasional sharp dissection or cautery. The dissection is performed carefully to avoid recurrence by ensuring the sac is dissected well back onto the cord structures.

The surgeon uses blunt and sharp dissection to free the peritoneal hernia sac from the cord structures. The mesh is inserted and secured to the posterior aspect of the muscle using ProGrip anatomic mesh, which adheres without sutures due to its velcro-like backing. The mesh is further secured with a 2-0 absorbable V-lock suture, with barbs ensuring secure placement and a loop allowing knotless initial throws. Additional stitches are placed along the medial aspect, ensuring a secure bite of Cooper's ligament.

The surgeon inserts the ProGrip anatomic mesh with a velcro-like backing, securing it to the posterior aspect of the muscle without sutures. The mesh is then sutured using a 2-0 absorbable V-Lock suture, with barbs for secure placement and a loop for knotless first throw. Additional stitches are run up the medial aspect, ensuring a secure bite of Cooper's ligament by pulling on the suture.

The surgeon uses wristed instruments from the XI robot to close the peritoneum with a continuous three-oh V-lock suture, ensuring the peritoneum is laid flat to prevent gapping and potential internal hernia formation. Interrupted sutures can be placed along the conjoined tendon or around the superior edge of the mesh as preferred.

The surgeon uses a three-oh V-lock suture to close the peritoneum with the wristed instruments of the XI robot, ensuring a continuous closure to prevent internal hernias. The suture is run along the conjoined tendon or around the superior edge of the mesh, laid flat, and completed without a knot by backing up a few throws to lock the suture.
The surgeon completes the suture line using a V-lock suture, securing it without a knot by backing up a few throws. The operation concludes after the suture line is finished.

This operation took only approximately twenty minutes skin to skin and cost significantly less because it avoids costly laparoscopic equipment such as tackers or balloon dissectors. The XI robot provides an excellent option for minimally invasive inguinal hernia repair. For more information about this or other general surgical robotic techniques, visit clarkgerhart.com.
